sc_blurb: While others may praise or criticize the Buddha, they tend to focus on trivial details. The Buddha presents an analysis of 62 kinds of wrong view, seeing through which one becomes detached from meaningless speculations.

sc_eng_sutta: The Great Discourse on Traces Left Behind
sc_blurb: The Buddha teaches about the six Buddhas of the past, and tells a lengthy account of one of those, Vipassī.
When Sunakkhatta threatens to disrobe, the Buddha is unimpressed. Rejecting showy displays of asceticism or wondrous powers, he demonstrates his pre-eminence.

The Buddha examines how the notion of a permanent self emerges from the process of perception. A wide range of phenomena are considered, embracing both naturalistic and cosmological dimensions. An unawakened person interprets experience in terms of a self, while those more advanced have the same experiences without attachment.

The Buddha examines how the notion of a permanent self emerges from the process of perception. A wide range of phenomena are considered, embracing both naturalistic and cosmological dimensions. An unawakened person interprets experience in terms of a self, while those more advanced have the same experiences without attachment.

sc_eng_sutta: The Shorter Discourse on the Lion’s Roar
sc_blurb: The Buddha declares that only those following his path can genuinely experience the four stages of awakening. This is because, while much is shared with other systems, none of them go so far as to fully reject all attachment to the idea of a self.

A discourse full of vibrant and memorable similes, on the importance of patience and love even when faced with abuse and criticism. The Buddha finishes with the simile of the saw, one of the most memorable similes found in the discourses.

The Buddha comes across three mendicants practicing diligently and harmoniously, and asks them how they do it. Reluctant to disclose their higher attainments, they explain how they deal with the practical affairs of living together. But when pressed by the Buddha, they reveal their meditation attainments.

The Buddha explains to a group of brahmins the conduct leading to rebirth in higher or lower states, including detailed explanations of the ten core practices which lay people should undertake, and which also form the basis for liberation.

The Buddha discusses mindfulness meditation with lay practitioners. Contrasting the openness of animals with the duplicity of humans, he explains how to practice in a way that causes no harm to oneself or others.

The Buddha discusses mindfulness meditation with lay practitioners. Contrasting the openness of animals with the duplicity of humans, he explains how to practice in a way that causes no harm to oneself or others.
Using the “object lesson” of a cup of water, the Buddha explains to his son, Rāhula, the importance of telling the truth and reflecting on one’s motives.

sc_eng_sutta: To Vacchagotta on the Three Knowledges
sc_blurb: The Buddha denies being omniscient, and sets forth the three higher knowledges that form the core of his awakened insight.

The Buddha relates an unusual account of a past life in the time of the previous Buddha, Kassapa. At that time he was not interested in Dhamma, and had to be forced to go see the Buddha. This discourse is important in understanding the development of the Bodhisattva doctrine.

The oldest and most respected brahmin of the age sends a student to examine the Buddha, and he spends several months following his every move before reporting back. Convinced that the Buddha fulfills an ancient prophecy of the Great Man, the brahmin becomes his disciple.

The Buddha tackles a group of Jain ascetics, pressing them on their claim to be practicing to end all suffering by self-mortification. He points out a series of fallacies in their logic, and explains his own middle way.

The Buddha tackles a group of Jain ascetics, pressing them on their claim to be practicing to end all suffering by self-mortification. He points out a series of fallacies in their logic, and explains his own middle way.
The Buddha describes in technical detail the process of insight of Venerable Sāriputta. Many ideas and terms in this text anticipate the Abhidhamma.
The Buddha describes his own practice of the meditation on emptiness.
This discourse opens with a short but powerful set of verses extolling the benefits of insight into the here and now, followed by an explanation.
As the great lay disciple Anāthapiṇḍika lies dying, Venerable Sāriputta visits him and gives a powerful teaching on non-attachment.
The Buddha crossed the flood of suffering by neither standing nor swimming.
The famous twelve links of dependent origination are spelled out, showing the origin and cessation of suffering dependent on ignorance.

The householder Nakulapitā asks the Buddha for help in coping with old age. The Buddha says to reflect: “Even though I am afflicted in body, my mind will be unafflicted.” Later Sāriputta explains this in terms of the five aggregates.
The six interior sense fields are impermanent, suffering, and not-self. When a noble disciple truly sees them like this, they become disillusioned and liberated.
Following ignorance comes the wrong eightfold path. But the right eightfold path follows true knowledge.
The Buddha crossed the flood of suffering by neither standing nor swimming.
A deity named Kassapa comes to the Buddha and speaks of the training of a mendicant in seclusion and right speech.

King Pasenadi meets the Buddha for the first time. He wonders how the Buddha can claim to be awakened when he is still so young. The Buddha teaches him four things that should not be looked down on for their youth.
Māra accuses the Buddha of having abandoned the path of true austerity.

The nun Āḷavikā has entered the Dark Wood seeking seclusion when Māra comes to threaten her, claiming there is no escape and no point in seclusion. She recognizes him instantly and dismisses his taunts, asserting that she is beyond his control.

After his awakening, the Buddha hesitated to teach, thinking that the Dhamma is too subtle for people to understand. But the high divinity Brahmā Sahampati appears and encourages him to teach, pointing out that there are some with “little dust in their eyes” who will understand.
When a brahmin lady expresses her confidence in the Triple Gem, her husband is annoyed. She encourages him to visit the Buddha, who soothes his anger.
Enticed by the sight of beautiful women, the newly ordained monk Vaṅgīsa dispels desire and celebrates his commitment to the spiritual path in verse.
A monk in the forest lets his mind drift to thoughts of the lay life, but is warned by a local deity.
A local spirit asks the Buddha how a body is produced if there is no soul.

When Sakka, the Lord of Gods, tries to organize a counterattack against the demons, he is frustrated by his lazy general Suvīra. Sakka asks Suvīra how one might get what one wants without effort; but Suvīra apparently believes Sakka can grant any wish he wants. The Buddha emphasizes that if effort is valuable among the gods, how much more so for mendicants.
The famous twelve links of dependent origination are spelled out, showing the origin and cessation of suffering dependent on ignorance.
For someone who has seen the truth, the suffering eliminated is like the great earth; what remains is like the dirt under a fingernail.
The senses function due to the various different elements of sense experience, namely, the internal sense organ, the external sense stimulus, and consciousness.
Transmigration is endless, like the grass and twigs in India.
Kassapa is content with robes, alms-food, lodging, and medicines.
Material possessions, honor, and praise are terrible things, obstructing spiritual progress.
The Buddha answers Rāhula’s request by teaching on the internal sense organs in light of the three characteristics.

While walking for alms down Vulture’s Peak, Venerable Moggallāna smiled at something invisible. Later, he told the Buddha it was a skeleton flying through the air, being pecked at by vultures. The Buddha confirmed that the man he had seen had been a butcher in his past life
As rafters depend on the roof-peak, unwholesome qualities depend on ignorance.
Moggallāna reflects that second absorption is the true noble silence, and the Buddha encourages him to develop it.

The householder Nakulapitā asks the Buddha for help in coping with old age. The Buddha says to reflect: “Even though I am afflicted in body, my mind will be unafflicted.” Later Sāriputta explains this in terms of the five aggregates.
Rādha asks the Buddha about Māra, and the Buddha explains in terms of the five aggregates. Seeing in this way leads to freedom.

The Buddha mentions a strange view to the effect that all changing phenomena of the world are really still and motionless. Such a view arises from grasping to the five aggregates. But by seeing the aggregates, as well as what is seen, heard, thought, and known, as impermanent, such a view is given up and one becomes a stream-enterer.

One with faith in the teachings on the six interior sense fields is called a “follower by faith”, while someone with conceptual understanding is called a “follower of the teachings”. But someone who sees them directly is called a stream-enterer.
The arising of the six interior sense fields is the arising of suffering.
Desire for six interior sense fields is a corruption of the mind, and letting go such desire one is able to realize the various kinds of direct knowledge.
Sāriputta goes to Mirkwood for meditation, and afterwards Ānanda asks why his face is so radiant. Sāriputta replies that he was practicing first absorption without any thought of self.
Dragons may be born from eggs, the womb, moisture, or spontaneously.
Phoenixes may be born from eggs, the womb, moisture, or spontaneously.
Centaurs may live in fragrant roots and other vegetation.
Cloud gods may live in cool clouds, warm clouds, storm clouds, wind clouds, or rain clouds.
The wanderer Vacchagotta asks the Buddha why the various speculative views come to be. The Buddha replies that it is because of not knowing form.
The six interior sense fields are impermanent, suffering, and not-self. When a noble disciple truly sees them like this, they become disillusioned and liberated.
There are three feelings, and a meditator practicing immersion can understand them.
Five things that make a woman either agreeable or disagreeable to men.
The wanderer Jambukhādaka asks Sāriputta about extinguishment and the path to realize it.

sc_eng_sutta: A Question About the First Absorption
sc_blurb: Moggallāna speaks to the mendicants of a time when his practice of the first absorption was faltering. He expresses his gratitude for the Buddha, who urged him to stabilize that attainment.

Citta the householder hears of a conversation among the monks regarding the distinction between the fetters and the things that are fettered. He goes to ask them on this topic, but they are unable to give a satisfactory answer, so he explained it to them. It is desire that is the fetter, like the yoke that binds two oxen.
A headman known for his aggressive nature approaches the Buddha and asks how to give up irritability. The Buddha points out that such reactions are fueled by greed, hate, and delusion.

While staying in Toraṇavatthu, King Pasenadi wishes to visit a spiritual teacher, and the nun Khemā is highly recommended to him. He asks her about whether a realized one still exists after death, and she says this is not answerable. Later he visits the Buddha, who replies in exactly the same way.
Following ignorance comes the wrong eightfold path. But the right eightfold path follows true knowledge.
Dragons nurture their strength in the Himalayas, then enter the rivers and reach the ocean. So too, a mendicant nurtures ethics and then develops the seven awakening factors.
At Ambapālī’s mango grove, the Buddha teaches the mendicants about the “way to convergence”, the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
The five faculties are faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom.
The four bases of psychic power lead from the near to the far shore.

While meditating alone, Anuruddha reflected that one who has neglected the four kinds of mindfulness meditation has neglected the path to the end of suffering. Moggallāna reads his mind and visits to question him on this. Anuruddha speaks of developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation internally and externally, in terms of origin and cessation, and developing power over perceptions.
Breath meditation is very beneficial. It is developed by going into seclusion, establishing mindfulness on the breath, and proceeding through sixteen stages.

Even a universal monarch may have a bad rebirth, but someone who has the four factors of stream-entry—experiential faith in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and ethical conduct—is freed from such destinies.
Mendicants should develop immersion so as to understand the four noble truths: suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path.
One who advances far along the path sloughs off the near shore and far, like a snake who sloughs off its skin.
The Buddha crossed the flood of suffering by neither standing nor swimming.
Even the divine Garden of Delight is impermanent.
A mendicant should live as if their head was on fire.
A series of deities give verses praising association with the good, and the Buddha gives his own response.
A deity recites to the Buddha some verses
Morality is good even in old age.
“Name” weighs everything down.
The killing of anger is the only killing praised by the noble ones.
A deity named Kassapa comes to the Buddha and speaks of the training of a mendicant in seclusion and right speech.
The deity Candimasa says that those who practice absorption will surely find escape. The Buddha adds that they must also be diligent and discard flaws.

The deity Siva says one should associate only with the good, and this will lead to happiness. The Buddha adds that associating with the good leads to the end of suffering. Siva is the Pali spelling of Sanskrit Śiva, and this marks a rare appearance of a god of this name in the early texts.

King Pasenadi meets the Buddha for the first time. He wonders how the Buddha can claim to be awakened when he is still so young. The Buddha teaches him four things that should not be looked down on for their youth.

A diverse group of ascetics passes by, and Pasenadi asks the Buddha if any of them are perfected ones. The Buddha says it is not easy for one such as Pasenadi to judge the spiritual achievements of others. Pasenadi reveals that the men are, in fact, his spies.
The Buddha teaches Pasenadi of four kinds of person, going from dark to dark, dark to light, light to dark, and light to light.
Māra accuses the Buddha of having abandoned the path of true austerity.
Māra smashes boulders in an attempt to scare the Buddha.
Māra approaches some young mendicants as an old brahmin, and tries to tempt them with talk of the pleasures of youth.

The nun Āḷavikā has entered the Dark Wood seeking seclusion when Māra comes to threaten her, claiming there is no escape and no point in seclusion. She recognizes him instantly and dismisses his taunts, asserting that she is beyond his control.

After his awakening, the Buddha hesitated to teach, thinking that the Dhamma is too subtle for people to understand. But the high divinity Brahmā Sahampati appears and encourages him to teach, pointing out that there are some with “little dust in their eyes” who will understand.
Brahmā Sanaṅkumāra appears to the Buddha and asserts that the aristocrat or khattiya is the best caste, but the Buddha is the best of people.
When a brahmin lady expresses her confidence in the Triple Gem, her husband is annoyed. She encourages him to visit the Buddha, who soothes his anger.
A brahmin farmer criticizes the Buddha for failing to produce food, merely living off the work of others. But the Buddha shows that his true work is spiritual.
Enticed by the sight of beautiful women, the newly ordained monk Vaṅgīsa dispels desire and celebrates his commitment to the spiritual path in verse.
A monk in the forest lets his mind drift to thoughts of the lay life, but is warned by a local deity.
A local spirit asks the Buddha how a body is produced if there is no soul.

When Sakka, the Lord of Gods, tries to organize a counterattack against the demons, he is frustrated by his lazy general Suvīra. Sakka asks Suvīra how one might get what one wants without effort; but Suvīra apparently believes Sakka can grant any wish he wants. The Buddha emphasizes that if effort is valuable among the gods, how much more so for mendicants.
Sakka achieved his status because of seven vows he kept in the past.
Anger is the only thing whose slaying the Buddha commends.
The famous twelve links of dependent origination are spelled out, showing the origin and cessation of suffering dependent on ignorance.

The Buddha defines the four kinds of “food” or “nutriment”, which include edible food, contact, intention, and consciousness. These are all produced by craving, and hence connect with dependent origination.
The Buddha’s teaching rests on his possession of ten powers and four grounds for self-confidence.

The Buddha asks Sāriputta to explain a verse from “The Questions of Ajita” in the Parāyana (Snp 5.2). At first, Sāriputta hesitates, but proceeds when the Buddha indicates he is looking for an answer in terms of conditionality.
A noble disciple who is a layperson has eliminated the fear that comes from breaking precepts, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and understands dependent origination.

A mendicant should thoroughly investigate the causes of suffering in accordance with dependent origination. If someone who still has ignorance makes a choice, their consciousness fares on to a suitable state of existence. But one who has eradicated ignorance is detached and is not reborn anywhere.

An ignorant person might become free of attachment to their body, but not their mind. Still, it would be better to attach to the body, as it is less changeable than the mind, which jumps about like a monkey.
One who does not understand aging and death is no true ascetic.
The many treasures to be found in the Triple Gem.
The senses function due to the various different elements of sense experience, namely, the internal sense organ, the external sense stimulus, and consciousness.

A rare group of seven elements: light, beauty, the four formless states, and the attainment of cessation. Each of these is known due to the negation of something: light vs. darkness, beauty vs. ugliness, and so on.
Those of bad qualities associate together, while those of good qualities associate together.
The four material elements of earth, water, heat, and air. Note that “air” is close in meaning to “wind”.
Transmigration is endless, like the grass and twigs in India.
When you see someone suffer, know that you too have experienced that.
Kassapa is content with robes, alms-food, lodging, and medicines.
Material possessions, honor, and praise are terrible things, obstructing spiritual progress.
Material possessions, honor, and praise are terrible things, which can corrupt the mind even of a pure mendicant.
Material possessions, honor, and praise can be even more tempting than a woman.
Devadatta provoked a schism due to material possessions, honor, and praise.
The Buddha answers Rāhula’s request by teaching on the internal sense organs in light of the three characteristics.
The Buddha teaches Rāhula about the internal sense organs in light of the three characteristics.

While walking for alms down Vulture’s Peak, Venerable Moggallāna smiled at something invisible. Later, he told the Buddha it was a skeleton flying through the air, being pecked at by vultures. The Buddha confirmed that the man he had seen had been a butcher in his past life

The householder Nakulapitā asks the Buddha for help in coping with old age. The Buddha says to reflect: “Even though I am afflicted in body, my mind will be unafflicted.” Later Sāriputta explains this in terms of the five aggregates.
Because the aggregates are impermanent, a noble disciple becomes disillusioned with them.
The aggregates are the burden, a person carries that burden, craving takes up the burden, and letting go puts down the burden.
Let go of what is not yours: the five aggregates. You wouldn’t be upset if someone took the grass and sticks from the monastery grounds, so why worry over the aggregates?
Dwell with yourself as an island and refuge, with the Dhamma as an island and refuge. How? By investigating how suffering arises from identification with the aggregates.
Consciousness stands dependent on the other four aggregates, and this attachment is what fuels the cycle of rebirth.
A mendicant comes to the Buddha to ask for instructions before going on retreat. The Buddha teaches them that in clinging to the aggregates one is bound by Māra.
Only a noble disciple truly understand the five aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape.
Ānanda praises Venerable Puṇṇa Mantāniputta, and says that it was when hearing his teaching on the aggregates that he understood the Dhamma.
If you grasp at the aggregates as a self, you will meet with calamity, like a man swept down by a mountain river, grasping at grass or rushes.
The aggregates considered in light of the four noble truths, phrased as “portions” or “sides”.
Ignorance is not understanding the aggregates.
The Buddha explains to a mendicant that ignorance is not understanding the aggregates in terms of arising and ceasing, while knowledge is understanding the aggregates.
The aggregates are like burning chaff.
Pleasure and pain arise inside oneself due to grasping the aggregates.
Rādha asks the Buddha about Māra, and the Buddha explains in terms of the five aggregates. Seeing in this way leads to freedom.
Rādha asks the Buddha about Māra, and the Buddha explains in terms of the five aggregates.

The Buddha mentions a strange view to the effect that all changing phenomena of the world are really still and motionless. Such a view arises from grasping to the five aggregates. But by seeing the aggregates, as well as what is seen, heard, thought, and known, as impermanent, such a view is given up and one becomes a stream-enterer.

The Buddha mentions a strange view to the effect that all changing phenomena of the world are really still and motionless. Such a view arises from grasping to the five aggregates, which are suffering. But by seeing the aggregates as impermanent, such a view is given up.

One with faith in the teachings on the six interior sense fields is called a “follower by faith”, while someone with conceptual understanding is called a “follower of the teachings”. But someone who sees them directly is called a stream-enterer.
The arising of the six interior sense fields is the arising of suffering.
The young Bodhisatta (Buddha-to-be), soon after leaving home, explains why he refuses King Bimbisāra’s offer of a position in his court.
Desire for six interior sense fields is a corruption of the mind, and letting go such desire one is able to realize the various kinds of direct knowledge.
Sāriputta goes to Mirkwood for meditation, and afterwards Ānanda asks why his face is so radiant. Sāriputta replies that he was practicing first absorption without any thought of self.
Phoenixes may be born from eggs, the womb, moisture, or spontaneously.
Centaurs may live in fragrant roots and other vegetation.
Cloud gods may live in cool clouds, warm clouds, storm clouds, wind clouds, or rain clouds.
The wanderer Vacchagotta asks the Buddha why the various speculative views come to be. The Buddha replies that it is because of not knowing form.
The six interior sense fields are impermanent, suffering, and not-self. When a noble disciple truly sees them like this, they become disillusioned and liberated.
The Buddha did not claim to be awakened until he had fully understood the gratification, drawback, and escape regarding the six interior sense fields.
The “all” consists of the six interior and exterior sense fields.

sc_eng_sutta: Ten on Liable to Be Reborn, Etc.

sc_blurb: A mendicant asks the Buddha how to see so as to give up ignorance. The Buddha says one should see the six interior and exterior sense fields as impermanent.

Venerable Migajāla asks how one lives alone, and how with a partner. The Buddha says that so long as one is bound by desire to the senses, one lives with a partner. A mendicant free of such desire dwells alone, even if they live in close association with worldly people.
Hearing that a newly-ordained mendicant was sick, the Buddha visited him to offer support and Dhamma encouragement.
The Buddha tells Ānanda that in the training of the noble one the “world” consisting of six sense experience is liable to wear out.
The six fields of contact lead to suffering if left unguarded, but if guarded lead to happiness. A lengthy set of verses illustrate this.
Someone who has given up attraction to the senses has arrived at the sanctuary.
A mendicant who craves the senses is under the sway of Māra.
At the Great Wood, the householder Ugga of Vesālī asks the Buddha why some become awakened in this very life, while others do not.
Not all mendicants have completed their work with regards to the six senses, but some of them have.
The six interior sense fields are not yours, but are old kamma, generated by choices in past lives. This is explained in terms of the four noble truths.

sc_eng_sutta: The Interior and the End of Relishing
sc_blurb: Right view is seeing the interior sense fields as they are, impermanent, and this leads to freedom.
In the Buddha’s teaching the “ocean” is the sense organs, the current is the sense objects.
The Buddha strings a series of powerful similes—vipers, murderers, an empty village, and more—into an extended parable of the dangers of existence and the way of escape.
There are three feelings, and a meditator practicing immersion can understand them.
A mendicant wonders how there can be three kinds of feeling, yet all of them are suffering.
When the wanderer Moḷiyasīvaka asks if all feelings are caused by karma in past lives. The Buddha denies this, asserting that feelings have many different causes.
Five things that make a woman either agreeable or disagreeable to men.
A woman with five good qualities has a good rebirth.
A woman with five powers lives confident at home.
The wanderer Jambukhādaka asks Sāriputta about extinguishment and the path to realize it.
The drawbacks of sensual desires.

sc_eng_sutta: A Question About the First Absorption
sc_blurb: Moggallāna speaks to the mendicants of a time when his practice of the first absorption was faltering. He expresses his gratitude for the Buddha, who urged him to stabilize that attainment.

Citta the householder hears of a conversation among the monks regarding the distinction between the fetters and the things that are fettered. He goes to ask them on this topic, but they are unable to give a satisfactory answer, so he explained it to them. It is desire that is the fetter, like the yoke that binds two oxen.
A headman known for his aggressive nature approaches the Buddha and asks how to give up irritability. The Buddha points out that such reactions are fueled by greed, hate, and delusion.

While staying in Toraṇavatthu, King Pasenadi wishes to visit a spiritual teacher, and the nun Khemā is highly recommended to him. He asks her about whether a realized one still exists after death, and she says this is not answerable. Later he visits the Buddha, who replies in exactly the same way.
Following ignorance comes the wrong eightfold path. But the right eightfold path follows true knowledge.
The Buddha goes on retreat for a fortnight. When he emerges, he describes his meditation on the conditionality of the eightfold path, which was similar to a practice he did shortly after awakening.
The wrong eightfold path is a wrong state; the right eightfold path is a right state.
The wrong eightfold path is wrong practice; the right eightfold path is right practice.
If others ask why you follow the spiritual path, say it’s for the fading away of lust.
As the dawn precedes the sun, good friendship precedes the eightfold path.
Dragons nurture their strength in the Himalayas, then enter the rivers and reach the ocean. So too, a mendicant nurtures ethics and then develops the seven awakening factors.
Just as living creatures are based on the earth, the awakening factors are based on ethics.
They’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening.
Skillful qualities are rooted in diligence, which promotes the awakening factors.
Anyone who abandons the three kinds of discrimination does so due to the awakening factors.
The Buddha spells out in detail the factors that nourish the hindrances, and those that nourish the awakening factors.
A series of passages on the benefits of meditating on a skeleton. Some editions treat the sections as separate suttas.

sc_eng_sutta: More on the Ganges River, Etc.
Another Series on a Realized One, Etc.

sc_blurb: At Ambapālī’s mango grove, the Buddha teaches the mendicants about the “way to convergence”, the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
Sāriputta asks the Buddha what is a great man, and the Buddha says it is someone whose mind has been freed by developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
Venerable Bhadda asks Ānanda about the ethical virtues encouraged by the Buddha—what is their purpose? To develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
The full knowledge of mindfulness meditation was not learned before the Buddha.
Develop the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, don’t let the deathless be lost.

sc_eng_sutta: Twelve Discourses on the Ganges River, Etc.

sc_blurb: The five faculties are faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom.
The Buddha explains the five faculties in terms of how they are obtained.
The Buddha did not claim to be awakened until he realized the five faculties.
Five faculties: pleasure, pain, happiness, sadness, and equanimity.
When Ānanda sees the Buddha’s sense faculties fading, the Buddha speaks on the decrepitude of old age, including verses.
Just as the lion is the king of beasts, wisdom is the chief of qualities that lead to awakening.
The four bases of psychic power lead from the near to the far shore.
The Buddha recounts the process of inquiry that lead to him fully understanding the four bases of psychic power, as well as the powers that result from that.
Before his awakening, the Buddha reflected on the path for developing the bases of psychic power.

While meditating alone, Anuruddha reflected that one who has neglected the four kinds of mindfulness meditation has neglected the path to the end of suffering. Moggallāna reads his mind and visits to question him on this. Anuruddha speaks of developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation internally and externally, in terms of origin and cessation, and developing power over perceptions.
By developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation Anuruddha surveys a thousand world systems.
Breath meditation is very beneficial. It is developed by going into seclusion, establishing mindfulness on the breath, and proceeding through sixteen stages.
The Buddha goes on a three month retreat, and later says that during that time he mostly practiced breath meditation.
A brahman teacher sends his students to the Buddha to see if the latter is truly awakened.

Even a universal monarch may have a bad rebirth, but someone who has the four factors of stream-entry—experiential faith in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and ethical conduct—is freed from such destinies.

sc_eng_sutta: A Saṅgha of a Thousand Nuns
sc_blurb: The Buddha teaches a thousand nuns the four factors of stream-entry.

Mahānāma the Sakyan expresses his fear that if he dies unmindful he may go to a bad rebirth. The Buddha tells him not to fear, as he will definitely go to a good place, since he has long practiced the Dhamma.
The four factors of stream-entry—with ethics as the fourth—are streams of merit.
The four factors of stream-entry—with ethics as the fourth—are streams of merit, and like the ocean cannot be counted. With verses.
A noble disciple with the four factors of stream-entry is a stream-enterer. With verses.
Mendicants should develop immersion so as to understand the four noble truths: suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path.

sc_eng_sutta: Rolling Forth the Wheel of Dhamma
sc_blurb: The famous first discourse, taught at Varanasi to the group of five ascetics. It begins by rejecting the extremes of asceticism and indulgence and recommends the middle way of the eightfold path. Then it defines the four noble truths and analyzes them in twelve aspects. It ends with Venerable Kondañña becoming the first person apart from the Buddha to realize the Dhamma.
It is because of not understanding the four noble truths that both the Buddha and others have wandered for so long in transmigration. With verses.
In a siṁsapā grove, the Buddha holds up a handful of leaves, and compares it to all the leaves in the forest. So too, he has taught the four noble truths, which is only a fraction of what he knows.

The Buddha tells the curious tale of a man who sat by a lotus pond to speculate about the world, and saw an army enter a lotus stalk. He thought he was going mad. The Buddha said that no, the army was real. Nevertheless, you should not speculate about such things, but should reflect on the four noble truths.
As a bit of dirt under a nail compared to the whole earth, so is the suffering remaining to one who has seen the four noble truths.

sc_eng_sutta: Passing Away as Humans and Reborn in Hell

sc_blurb: Fools are dangerous, but the wise are safe.
What well-known mendicants should encourage.
Of various individuals on the path, which is best?
Parents should be honored like Brahmā.
Factors in making good karma.
You can begin to practice even late in life: advice to elderly brahmins.
The beliefs that everything is caused by past karma, by a creator God, or by chance all lead to inaction. The Buddha teaches dependent origination.

sc_eng_sutta: An Expert in the Monastic Law (1st)
sc_blurb: An expert in monastic conduct has seven qualities.

The brahmin Verañja rebukes the Buddha for his lack of respect for senior brahmins. He levels a series of criticisms, each of which the Buddha deflects by redefining terms. The Buddha affirms that his claim to superiority is because he was the first to achieve awakening.

sc_eng_sutta: The Sabbath With Eight Factors, In Brief
sc_blurb: When the sabbath is observed by following the eight precepts, one lives for that day like the perfected ones. Such a sabbath is glorious.
Mahāpajāpatī Gotamī, the Buddha’s foster mother, requests ordination from the Buddha. He declines, until urged to relent by Ānanda. He allows Mahāpajāpatī to go forth on eight conditions.
The Buddha describes eight cases where a mendicant has a desire for material things. Sometimes they fall under the sway of that desire, but sometimes they rise above it.

After the rains season, Sāriputta takes leave of the Buddha. Another monk falsely accuses Sāriputta of hitting him before leaving. The Buddha calls Sāriputta to answer the accusation. Before the Saṅgha, Sāriputta denies it, uttering a powerful statement of spiritual prowess.
A comparison of the people of India, of Uttarakuru, and of the heaven of the Thirty-Three.
